text: The surgeon inserts the wound protector portion of a single port access system through a three-centimeter fan-and-steel incision.
The surgeon utilizes a single port access system inserted through a three-centimeter fan and steel incision for the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon inserts the wound protector portion into the single port access system through the three-centimeter incision.
The surgeon utilizes a ten millimeter, thirty degree bariatric scope, which is forty-five millimeters long, to visualize the surgical field during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses straight instruments, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon holds a Park's grasper in the left hand and a ligature device in the right hand, preparing to perform the surgical procedure using these two instruments.
The surgeon holds a Park's grasper in the left hand and a ligature device in the right hand, preparing to manipulate tissue during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon identifies the ileocolic pedicle and begins a medial to lateral dissection, entering the retroperitoneal plane.
The surgeon identifies the ileocolic pedicle and begins a medial to lateral dissection, entering into the retroperitoneal plane.
The surgeon creates a window around the iliocolic pedicle and prepares to divide it using a ligature, with minimal intra-abdominal fat visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon has created a window around the ileocolic pedicle and is preparing to divide it using the ligature instrument.
The surgeon uses the Silsport single port access system to carefully cauterize tissue near the visible ureter, ensuring secure hemostasis to prevent potential bleeding complications.
The surgeon visually identifies the ureter during the dissection process.

The surgeon lifts the right colon mesentery away from the retroperitoneum, carefully dissecting the mesentery closer to the colon without performing high ligation, as the procedure addresses a benign disease.
The surgeon lifts the right colon mesentery away from the retroperitoneum, exposing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon continues to dissect the mesentery, moving closer to the colon, carefully separating the tissue from the surrounding structures.
The surgeon adjusts the port to change the angles of the instruments, facilitating the dissection. The duodenum is swept down, and the mesentery is taken without performing high ligations.
The surgeon adjusts the port, altering the angles of the instruments to facilitate the dissection of the right colon mesentery.
The surgeon sweeps the duodenum downward, separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon continues to dissect and take down the mesentery adjacent to the colon.
Using a thirty-degree camera, the surgeon adjusts the angle to visualize and sweep the duodenum down from the transverse colon mesentery, employing a hand-over-hand technique.
Using a thirty-degree camera, the surgeon adjusts the angle to visualize and sweep the duodenum down from the transverse colon mesentery.

The surgeon continues to sweep the duodenum down off the mesentery, preparing to take the transverse colon mesentery. The inferior vena cava (IVC) is visible, along with the kidney, highlighting the absence of retroperitoneal fat.
The surgeon continues to sweep the duodenum down off the transverse colon mesentery, preparing for the subsequent removal of the mesentery.
The surgeon points out the inferior vena cava (IVC) to the assistant, highlighting its location in the surgical field.
The surgeon points out the kidney, highlighting the absence of retroperitoneal fat in the surrounding area.
The surgeon identifies the right colic artery and applies triple cautery to ensure thorough hemostasis, while gently teasing the duodenum down from the mesentery using a single port approach.
The surgeon identifies the right colic artery and applies triple cauterization to the vessel.
The surgeon gently teases the duodenum down from the mesentery, preparing for further dissection of the transverse colon mesentery.
The surgical field is positioned approximately one centimeter above the pubic bone, indicating the location of the current operative focus.

The surgeon identifies and dissects the right branch of the middle colic artery, using straight instruments to approach the colon wall. The medial dissection of the right colon and hepatic flexure is completed, and the trajectory is adjusted for further dissection.
The surgeon identifies what appears to be the right branch of the middle colic artery during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon dissects and takes the right branch of the middle colic artery, approaching the colon wall.
The surgeon completes the medial dissection of the right colon and hepatic flexure, revealing the anatomical structures and tissue planes in this area.

The surgeon approaches the hepatic colic ligament, entering the dissection plane from below while staying close to the colon. The duodenum has been swept down, minimizing the risk of inadvertent thermal injury during the dissection.
The surgeon approaches the hepatic colic ligament and enters the dissection plane from below, preparing to separate the ligament from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon maintains close proximity to the colon wall during the dissection, ensuring precise manipulation of the tissue.
The surgeon sweeps the duodenum downward, ensuring it is clear from the dissection area near the colon.
The surgeon inadvertently burns tissue while preparing to enter the dissection plane near the hepatic colic ligament.

The surgeon enters the previously dissected medial plane, revealing visible bruising in the area. The hepatic flexure is approached, noting its high position, as the dissection continues around this anatomical structure.
The surgeon reveals bruising as the dissection plane near the hepatic colic ligament becomes visible, indicating progress in the dissection of the right colon and hepatic flexure.
The surgeon enters the previously dissected medial plane and maneuvers around the hepatic flexure of the colon.

The surgeon uses traction with the left hand while hugging the bowel wall with a ligature, taking down the lateral attachments to the hepatic flexure. The dissection continues along the white line down to the cecum, with the ureter visible in the surgical field. The surgeon leaves the cecal and terminal ileum attachments intact to prevent the colon from twisting or obstructing the view during patient position changes.
The surgeon uses traction with the left hand while closely following the bowel wall with the ligature device, maintaining proximity to the tissue during dissection.
The surgeon has taken down the lateral attachments to the hepatic flexure and is continuing the dissection along the white line, progressing towards the cecum.

The surgeon leaves the cecal and terminal ileum attachments intact to prevent the colon from twisting or obstructing the view during patient repositioning. The ureter is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon leaves the cecal attachments in place, maintaining stability of the colon during the procedure.

The surgeon proceeds to the next phase of the operation, taking the transverse colon mesentery along with the omentum. The thinness of the patient facilitates this process, as the surgeon carefully separates and removes the omentum from the surgical field.
The surgeon proceeds to the next phase of the operation, focusing on dissecting the transverse colon mesentery.
The surgeon uses a surgical instrument to take the transverse colon mesentery along with the omentum, applying traction to separate these structures.
The surgeon proceeds to take the omentum, which is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon maintains close proximity to the bowel wall while working on the long transverse colon mesentery. The camera is positioned super pubic, providing clear visualization and favorable angles for instrument manipulation. The patient is positioned in reverse Trendelenburg with the left shoulder elevated to enhance exposure as the surgeon approaches the hepatic flexure.
The surgeon navigates along the bowel wall, revealing the long transverse colon mesentery during the colectomy procedure.
The patient is positioned in reverse Trendelenburg with the left shoulder elevated to enhance surgical exposure.
The surgeon continues dissection along the bowel wall, approaching the flexure of the transverse colon.

The surgeon continues to dissect by staying close to the colon, first taking the omental attachments near the flexure separately from the mesentery. The omental attachments are removed, followed by the mesenteric attachments, allowing the tissue to be pulled down sequentially. The thicker tissues in this area require careful use of the ligature to ensure complete hemostasis and prevent bleeding.
The surgeon continues to dissect along the colon, maintaining close proximity to the bowel wall as they progress upward.
As the surgeon approaches the flexure, they carefully separate the omental attachments from the colon, distinct from the mesentery.
The surgeon has taken the omental attachments and is now dissecting the mesentery from the same segment of the colon.
The surgeon continues to pull down the colon sequentially, aided by the patient's positioning in reverse Trendelenburg with the left shoulder elevated.
The surgeon removes the omental attachments and proceeds to dissect the mesenteric attachments from the colon, maintaining close proximity to the colon during the procedure.

The surgeon carefully dissects the splenic flexure, removing attachments from the inferior border of the pancreas. The flexure is visibly lowered, and the surgeon proceeds to dissect the descending colon mesentery, maintaining close proximity to the bowel wall.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the bowel wall, gradually mobilizing the splenic flexure, which is nearly completely freed from its attachments.
The surgeon carefully dissects and removes attachments from the inferior border of the pancreas.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the splenic flexure, visibly detaching it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon proceeds to transect the mesentery of the descending colon, carefully separating it from the surrounding tissues.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers along the bowel wall, maintaining close proximity to ensure precise dissection.

The surgeon continues to dissect the mesentery along the descending colon towards the sigmoid colon, working from a superior to inferior angle. As the dissection progresses, the visual angles become less favorable, prompting the surgeon to take additional mesentery before switching techniques.

The surgeon approaches the descending colon from a superior to inferior angle, moving towards the sigmoid colon. The visual field becomes limited, affecting the ability to maintain optimal angles for dissection.
The surgeon continues to dissect and remove additional portions of the mesentery from the descending colon.

The surgeon adjusts the patient to Trendelenburg position and begins releasing the lateral attachments of the sigmoid colon. The dissection progresses from the rectosigmoid area back up towards the descending colon, addressing the challenging angle from the superior side. After completing the lateral dissection, the surgeon continues to mobilize the mesentery.
The surgeon places the patient in the Trendelenburg position and approaches from the rectosigmoid region, joining the dissection with the previously dissected area from the superior side.
The surgeon reaches a point in the dissection where the superior approach becomes challenging to follow, indicating a transition in the surgical view or technique.
The surgeon prepares to switch the surgical approach, likely repositioning instruments or adjusting the view to continue the dissection.
The surgeon releases the lateral attachments of the sigmoid colon, detaching it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon navigates back up towards the descending colon, retracing the path of previous dissection.
The surgeon approaches the surgical site from the inferior aspect, adjusting the angle to continue the procedure on the sigmoid colon.
After completing the lateral dissection, the surgeon continues to mobilize the mesentery, manipulating the tissue to free it from surrounding structures.

The surgeon creates a window in the distal sigmoid colon mesentery, close to the bowel wall, to safely dissect away from the ureter. The mesentery is progressively taken with careful bites, facilitated by prior lateral mobilization, until reaching the rectosigmoid junction.
The surgeon carefully unfolds the mesentery to maintain a safe distance from the ureter during the dissection.
The surgeon creates a window in the distal sigmoid colon, positioned close to the bowel wall, to access and dissect through the mesentery.
The surgeon identifies a clear window in the mesentery after taking a bite, providing a visible path for further dissection.
The surgeon takes another bite into the mesentery, continuing the dissection process to mobilize the tissue.
The surgeon uses the created window to continue dissecting and taking down the remaining sigmoid mesentery, ensuring careful separation from surrounding structures.
The surgeon approaches the sigmoid mesentery from one side, facilitating easier access for dissection.
The surgeon demonstrates how lateral mobilization has facilitated the easy manipulation and dissection of the sigmoid mesentery.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the colon mesentery, preparing to take additional bites to reach the rectosigmoid junction.

The surgeon has preserved the superior hemorrhoidal artery and is now addressing the lateral attachments of the cecum, terminal ileum, and appendix. The colon and small bowel are fully mobilized, allowing the surgeon to grasp the cecum and exteriorize it for division at the ileocolic junction. The medial dissection is complete, and the mesentery is mobilized to facilitate reach, similar to a J-pouch procedure.
The surgeon has preserved the superior hemorrhoidal artery during the procedure, ensuring its integrity while continuing the dissection.

The surgeon prepares to address the lateral attachments of the cecum, terminal ileum, and appendix, ensuring the colon and small bowel remain untwisted. After freeing these structures, the surgeon plans to exteriorize the cecum to divide the ileocolic junction.

The surgeon mobilizes a portion of the terminal ileum mesentery to improve reach, similar to techniques used in J-pouch procedures. The medial dissection is completed, and the colon is now fully freed from surrounding attachments.

The surgeon removes the cap from the port and uses a Babcock clamp to exteriorize the cecum and ileocolic junction. After clearing additional mesentery, the ileocolic junction is divided with a stapler. A stitch is placed on the ileum. The divided colon is exteriorized back to the rectosigmoid junction, which is cleaned of its mesentery, and division occurs in the upper rectum through a low Pfannenstiel incision.
The surgeon removes the cap from the port and uses a Babcock clamp to grasp and exteriorize the cecum, followed by the ileocolic junction.
The surgeon clears additional mesentery tissue and prepares to divide the ileocolic junction using a surgical stapler.
The surgeon places a stitch on the ileum, preparing for the next step in the procedure.

The surgeon has exteriorized the divided colon back to the rectus sigmoid junction, which is visibly cleaned of its mesentery. The division is occurring in the upper rectum, facilitated by a low Pfannenstiel incision.
The surgeon prepares to divide the rectum, having previously mobilized and exteriorized the colon and ileocolic junction.

The surgeon places the cap back on the port with sutures holding the two ends of the bowel, ensuring the mesentery is not twisted. The small bowel is positioned to the patient's left, with the mesentery extending straight from the duodenum to the cut edge of the ileum. The surgeon corrects a twist in the ileum, repositioning the loop of bowel under the mesentery to align the iliacolic pedicle and rectum.
The surgeon places the cap back on the port, securing the two ends of the bowel with sutures in preparation for the anastomosis.
The surgeon inspects the mesentery to ensure it is not twisted during the procedure, maintaining proper alignment of the tissue.
The surgeon positions the small bowel to the patient's left side, organizing the surgical field for the next steps of the procedure.
The surgeon examines the cut edge of the ileum, which is visible following the division of the rectum.
The surgeon carefully inspects the bowel to ensure there is no 360-degree twist in the mesentery before proceeding with the anastomosis.
The ileum is visibly twisted, indicating a slight rotation of the bowel segment.
The surgeon identifies a loop of bowel positioned beneath the mesentery, adjusting the orientation for proper visualization.
The surgeon repositions the bowel, revealing the complete cut edge of the small bowel mesentery extending from the duodenum downward.
The surgeon identifies the iliacolic pedicle, a key vascular structure in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the rectum, visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon completes a hand-sewn side-to-end anastomosis through the fan and steel incision, ensuring the mesentery is straight. The ileum is clamped, and the pelvis is flooded for a leak test, which is negative.
The surgeon performs a hand-sewn side-to-end anastomosis through the fan and steel incision, connecting the bowel segments.
The surgeon visually inspects the mesentery to ensure it is straight and properly aligned.
The surgeon clamps the ileum, preparing for further surgical manipulation.
The surgeon floods the pelvis with fluid to perform a leak test, checking for any visible leaks at the site of the hand-sewn side-to-end anastomosis.
The surgeon performs a leak test on the hand-sewn side-to-end anastomosis, confirming its integrity as the test is negative, and concludes the procedure.
